Description: Low power for organic material preservation
Low energy to preserve wood fibers
Fast scanning to prevent heat damage
Single gentle pass for delicate surfaces

Typical range for delicate organic materials like wood to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for controlled cleaning of light contaminants on Ebony. Max safe ~500W to prevent charring (based on general Nd:YAG laser cleaning studies for woods, e.g., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2018).

Effective speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for uniform Ebony surface prep (industry standards from IPG Photonics manuals and laser cleaning process optimization).

Nanosecond regime preferred for minimal heat-affected zone on low-conductivity woods like Ebony; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces thermal damage but increases cost. 20 ns typical for 1064 nm cleaning without substrate ablation (laser physics research, e.g., Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2020).

20-50 kHz balances speed and heat accumulation for organic materials; higher rates (>50 kHz) risk overheating Ebony due to poor thermal conductivity. Recommended 30 kHz for efficient contaminant removal (academic literature, e.g., Applied Surface Science, 2019).

Fluence below Ebony's ablation threshold (~5 J/cm² at 1064 nm for dark woods); 0.5-2 J/cm² for light cleaning, up to 4 for heavy. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² safe for contamination removal without fiber damage (ablation threshold studies, e.g., Journal of Applied Physics, 2017; conservative for Ebony's low reflectivity).

Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for fine detail on Ebony grain, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. Recommended 200 μm for precise industrial cleaning (equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems).

Single pass for light contaminants, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy or layered removal on woods. Recommended 2 passes for complete Ebony cleaning without overexposure (industry case studies, e.g., laser restoration of wooden artifacts in Heritage Science, 2021).

50-70% ensures uniform coverage and avoids gaps/striping on irregular surfaces like Ebony; lower for speed, higher for thoroughness. Recommended 60% for optimal process (process optimization studies, e.g., Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing, 2022).
